brief: Users can modify resources owned by other users by including an ownerId field in the request body.

root_cause.description: The resource update endpoint reads ownerId from req.body to perform ownership verification. An authenticated attacker can include an arbitrary ownerId in the PUT request payload to spoof ownership of any resource, bypassing the authorization check entirely.
root_cause.mechanism: ownerId sourced from untrusted req.body instead of authenticated req.user.id, allowing any user to claim ownership of any resource

required_findings.description: Agent must identify the resource controller/update endpoint as the failing subsystem
Agent must identify that ownerId is being read from the untrusted request body
Agent must identify that ownerId should come from req.user (the authenticated session) and that reading it from the body enables spoofing
Agent must identify resource.controller.ts line 45 as the exact location of the insecure ownerId read

anti_patterns.description: CORS and CSP headers control cross-origin browser access; they do not prevent an authenticated user from crafting a PUT request with an arbitrary body
Input sanitization and validation prevent malformed or malicious data values; the ownerId value itself is valid — the flaw is using it from the wrong source (body vs session)
The attacker uses their own valid session; no session hijacking is required because ownerId is accepted directly from the request body

notes: The fix is to replace `const { ownerId } = req.body` with `const ownerId = req.user.id` at line 45, and optionally to remove ownerId from the accepted body schema entirely to prevent confusion. A score-4 investigation will also note that a regression test should PUT with a mismatched ownerId in the body and assert a 403 response.
